title: Lower Bounds on Metropolized Sampling Methods for Well-Conditioned Distributions [PDF]

# **Lower Bounds on Metropolized Sampling Methods for Well-Conditioned Distributions**

**Yin Tat Lee** University of Washington and Microsoft Research `yintat@uw.edu`

**Ruoqi Shen** University of Washington `shenr3@cs.washington.edu`

**Kevin Tian** Stanford University `kjtian@stanford.edu`

## **Abstract**

We give lower bounds on the performance of two of the most popular sampling methods in practice, the Metropolis-adjusted Langevin algorithm (MALA) and multi-step Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) with a leapfrog integrator, when applied to well-conditioned distributions. Our main result is a nearly-tight lower bound of Ω([�] _κd_ ) on the mixing time of MALA from an exponentially warm start, matching a line of algorithmic results [DCWY18, CDWY19, LST20a] up to logarithmic factors and answering an open question of [CLA[+] 20]. We also show that a polynomial dependence on dimension is necessary for the relaxation time of HMC under any number of leapfrog steps, and bound the gains achievable by changing the step count. Our HMC analysis draws upon a novel connection between leapfrog integration and Chebyshev polynomials, which may be of independent interest.

## **1 Introduction**

Sampling from a continuous distribution in high dimensions is a fundamental problem in algorithm design. As sampling serves as a key subroutine in a variety of tasks in machine learning [AdFDJ03], statistical methods [RC99], and scientific computing [Liu01], it is an important undertaking to understand the complexity of sampling from families of distributions arising in applications.

The more restricted problem of sampling from a particular distribution family we call “wellconditioned” has garnered a substantial amount of recent research effort from the algorithmic learning and statistics communities. This specific family is interesting for a number of reasons. First, it is _practically relevant_ : Bayesian methods have found increasing use in machine learning applications [Bar12], and many distributions in these settings are well-conditioned, such as multivariate Gaussians, mixture models with small separation, and densities arising from Bayesian logistic regression with a Gaussian prior [DCWY18]. Moreover, for several widely-used sampler implementations in popular packages [Aba16, CGH[+] 17], such as the Metropolis-adjusted Langevin algorithm (MALA) and Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC), the target having a small condition number is in some sense a _minimal assumption for known provable guarantees_ (discussed more thoroughly in Section 1.2).

Finally, the highly-documented success of first-order (gradient) methods in optimization [Bec17], which are particularly favorable in the well-conditioned setting, has driven interest in connections between optimization and sampling. Exploring this connection has been highly fruitful: since seminal work of [JKO98], demonstrating that the Langevin dynamics which MALA and HMC discretize has an interpretation as gradient descent on density space, a flurry of work [Dal17, CCBJ18, DCWY18, DR18, DM19, DMM19, CDWY19, CV19, SL19, MMW[+] 19, LST20a, LST20b, CLA[+] 20] has ob-

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

tained improved upper bounds for the mixing of various Langevin discretizations for well-conditioned sampling. Many of these works draw inspiration from first-order optimization.

On the other hand, demonstrating _lower bounds_ on the complexity of sampling tasks (in the wellconditioned regime or otherwise) has proven remarkably challenging. To our knowledge, there are few unconditional lower bounds for sampling tasks. This is in stark contrast to the theory of optimization, where there are matching upper and lower bounds for a variety of problems and query models, such as convex optimization under first-order oracle access [Nes03]. This gap in the algorithmic theory of sampling is our primary motivation: we aim to answer the following more restricted question.
## _What is the complexity of the popular sampling methods, MALA and HMC, for sampling well-conditioned distributions?_

The problem we study is still less general than _unconditional query lower bounds_ for sampling, in that our lower bounds are _algorithm-specific_ ; we characterize the performance of particular sampling algorithms on a distribution family. However, we believe asking this question, and developing an understanding of it, is an important first step towards a theory of complexity for sampling. On the one hand, algorithm-specific lower bounds highlight their weaknesses, pinpointing bottlenecks in attaining faster rates; this is useful for algorithm designers, as it clarifies what key barriers must be overcome. On the other hand, the hard instances which arise in designing lower bounds may have important structural properties, paving the way to stronger and more general bounds.

For these reasons, we focus on characterizing the complexity of MALA and HMC (see Section 2 for algorithm definitions), which are often the samplers of choice in practice, by lower bounding their performance when they are used to sample from densities proportional to exp( _−f_ ( _x_ )), where _f_ : R _[d] →_ R has a finite condition number _κ < ∞_ .[1] We call such a density “well-conditioned.” Finally, we explicitly assume throughout that _κ_ = _O_ ( _d_[4] ), as otherwise in light of our lower bounds the general-purpose logconcave samplers of [LV07, JLLV20, Che21] are preferable.

## **1.1 Our results**

Our primary contribution is a nearly-tight characterization of the performance of MALA for sampling from two high-dimensional distribution families without a warm start assumption: well-conditioned Gaussians, and the more general family of well-conditioned densities. We prove the following two lower bounds on MALA’s complexity, which is a one-parameter algorithm (for a given target distribution) depending only on step size. We fix a scale [1 _, κ_ ] on the problem, as otherwise nonscale-invariance can be exploited to give more trivial lower bounds (cf. Appendix B, supplement).

**Theorem 1.** _For every step size, there is a target Gaussian on_ R _[d] whose negative log-density always_ ~~_√_~~ _d has Hessian eigenvalues in_ [1 _, κ_ ] _, such that the relaxation time of MALA is_ Ω( ~~_√_~~ _[κ]_ log _d_[)] _[.]_ **Theorem 2.** _For every step size, there is a target density on_ R _[d] whose negative log-density always has Hessian eigenvalues in_ [1 _, κ_ ] _, such that the relaxation time of MALA is_ Ω( log _[κd] d_[)] _[.]_

To give more context, MALA is a Metropolis-adjusted Markov chain, which performs updates preserving the stationary distribution. It can be derived by applying a filter on the Euler discretization of the Langevin dynamics, a stochastic differential equation with stationary density _∝_ exp( _−f_ ( _x_ )):

where _Wt_ is Brownian motion. Such _Metropolis-adjusted_ methods typically provide total variation distance guarantees, and attain logarithmic dependence on the target accuracy.[2] The mixing of such chains is governed by their relaxation time, also known as the inverse _spectral gap_ .

However, in continuous space, it is not always clear how to relate the relaxation time and _mixing time_ , the time it takes to reach total variation[1] _e_[to the stationary distribution from a given start (we]

We complement Theorems 1 and 2 with a _mixing time_ lower bound from an exponentially warm start. **Theorem 3.** _For every step size, there is a target density on_ R _[d] whose negative log-density always has Hessian eigenvalues in_ [1 _, κ_ ] _, such that MALA initialized at an_ exp( _d_ ) _-warm start requires_ Ω( log _κd_[2] _d_[)] _iterations to reach e[−]_[1] _total variation distance to the stationary distribution._

We remark that Theorem 3 is the first _mixing time_ lower bound for discretizations of the Langevin dynamics we are aware of: other related bounds have primarily been on relaxation times [CV19, LST20a, CLA[+] 20]. It is unknown how to obtain a starting distribution for a general _κ_ -conditioned distribution with warmness better than _κ[d]_ (obtained by _N_ ( _x[∗] , L_[1] **[I]**[)][ where] _[ L]_[ is the smoothness and] _[ x][∗]_ is the mode).[3] A line of work [DCWY18, CDWY19, LST20a] analyzed the performance of MALA under this start, culminating in a mixing time of _O_[�] ( _κd_ ), where _O_[�] hides logarithmic factors in _κ_ , _d_ , and the target accuracy. On the other hand, a recent work [CLA[+] 20] demonstrated that MALA obtains a mixing time scaling as _O_[�] (poly( _κ_ ) _√d_ ), initialized at a _polynomially_ warm start,[4] and further showed that such a mixing time is tight (in its dependence on _d_ ). They posed as an open question whether it was possible to obtain _O_[�] (poly( _κ_ ) _d_[1] _[−]_[Ω(1)] ) mixing from an explicit start.

We address this question via Theorem 3, showing the _O_[�] ( _κd_ ) rate of [LST20a] for MALA applied to a _κ_ -conditioned density is nearly tight. To prove Theorems 1-3, in each case we exhibit an exp( _−d_ )-sized set according to the stationary measure where either the chain cannot move in poly( _d_ ) steps, or must choose a very small step size. Beyond exhibiting a mixing bound, this demonstrates the subexponential warmness assumption in [CLA[+] 20] is necessary for their improved bound. To our knowledge, this is the first _nearly-tight_ characterization of a specific sampler’s performance, and improves bounds of [CLA[+] 20, LST20a]. It also implies going beyond _O_[�] ( _κd_ ) mixing for general well-conditioned densities requires subexponential warmness.
The lower bound statement of Theorem 3 is warmness-sensitive, and is of the following (somewhat non-standard) form: for _β_ = exp( _d_ ), we provide a lower bound on the quantity

In other words, we are allowed to choose both the hard density and starting distribution adaptively based on the algorithm parameters (in the case of MALA, our choices are in response to the step size). We note that this type of lower bound is compatible with standard conductance-based upper bound analyses, which typically only depend on the starting distribution through the warmness parameter.

We further study the multi-step generalization of MALA, known as Hamiltonian Monte Carlo with a leapfrog integrator (which we refer to as HMC). In addition to a step size _η_ , HMC is parameterized by a number of steps per iteration _K_ , making _K_ gradient queries in every step to perform a discretization of the Langevin dynamics, before applying a Metropolis filter. Multi-step HMC has the potential of attaining overall runtime gains by effectively taking longer steps. It was recently shown [CDWY19] that under higher derivative bounds, balancing _η_ and _K_ more carefully depending on problem parameters could break the apparent _κd_ barrier of MALA, even from an exponentially warm start.

It is natural to ask if there is a stopping point for improving HMC. We demonstrate that HMC cannot obtain a better relaxation time than _O_[�] ( _κ√dK[−]_[1] ) for any _K_ , even when the target is a Gaussian. Since every HMC step requires _K_ gradients, this suggests Ω([�] _κ√d_ ) queries are necessary. **Theorem 4.** _For every step size and count, there is a target Gaussian on_ R _[d] whose negative logκ_ ~~_√_~~ _d density always has Hessian eigenvalues in_ [1 _, κ_ ] _, such that the relaxation time of HMC is_ Ω( _K_ ~~_[√]_~~ log _d_[)] _[.]_

In Appendix B of the supplement, we also lower bound how much increasing _K_ can improve HMC in the in-between range _κ√d_ to _κd_ . In particular, we demonstrate that if _K ≤ d[c]_ for some constant _c_ , then _K_ -step HMC can only improve the relaxation time of Theorem 4 by roughly _K_[2] , showing that

Our mixing lower bound technique in Theorem 3 does not directly extend to give a complementary lower bound for Theorem 4 for all _K_ , but we defer this to interesting future work.

## **1.2 Prior work**

Sampling from well-conditioned distributions (as well those with stronger higher derivative bounds) is an extremely active and rich research area, so for brevity we focus on discussing two types of related work in this section: upper bounds for the MALA and HMC Markov chains, and lower bounds for sampling and related problems. We defer to e.g. [Dal17, CCBJ18, DCWY18, DR18, DM19, DMM19, CDWY19, CV19, SL19, MMW[+] 19, LST20a, LST20b, CLA[+] 20] and references therein for a more complete account on progress on well-conditioned sampling.

**Theory of MALA and HMC.** MALA was originally proposed in [Bes94], and subsequently its practical and theoretical performance has received extensive treatment in the literature (cf. [PSC[+] 15]). Several analyses related to the well-conditioned setting we study predate [DCWY18], e.g. [RT96, BRH12], but they consider more restricted settings or do not state explicit dependences on _κ_ and _d_ . Recently, a line of work has obtained a sequence of upper bounds on the mixing of MALA. First, [DCWY18] demonstrated a mixing time of _O_[�] ( _κd_ + _κ_[1] _[.]_[5] _[√] d_ ) from a polynomially warm start, and the same authors later proved the same bound under an explicit exponentially warm start [CDWY19]. Later, [LST20a] demonstrated that under an appropriate averaging scheme, the mixing time could be improved to _O_[�] ( _κd_ ) with no low-order dependence. Finally, [CLA[+] 20] recently demonstrated that from a polynomially warm start, MALA mixes in time _O_[�] (poly( _κ_ ) _√d_ ) for general _κ_ -conditioned distributions and in time _O_[�] (poly( _κ_ ) _[√]_ 3 _d_ ) for Gaussians, and posed the open question of attaining similar bounds from an explicit (exponentially) warm start, a primary motivation for our exploration.

HMC can be viewed as a multi-step generalization of MALA, with two parameters (a step size _η_ and a step count _K_ ); when _K_ = 1, it matches MALA exactly. For larger _K_ , the algorithm simulates the (continuous-time) Hamiltonian dynamics with respect to the potential _f_ ( _x_ ) +[1] 2 _[∥][v][∥]_ 2[2][where] _[ f]_[is] the target’s negative log-density and _v_ is an auxiliary “velocity;” intuitively, larger _K_ leads to more faithful discretizations. However, there are few explicit analyses of the (Metropolis-adjusted) HMC algorithm on well-conditioned distributions.[5] To our knowledge, the only upper bound for the mixing of HMC stronger than known analyses of its specialization MALA is by [CDWY19], which gave a suite of bounds balancing three parameters: the conditioning _κ_ , the dimension _d_ , and the _Hessian Lipschitz parameter LH_ , under an additional bounded third derivatives assumption. Supposing _LH_ is polynomially bounded by the smoothness _L_ , they demonstrate that HMC can sometimes achieve sublinear dependence on _d_ in number of gradient queries, where the improvement depends on _κ_ and 1 2 11 _d_ (e.g. if _κ ∈_ [ _d_ 3 _, d_ 3 ] and _LH ≤ L_[1] _[.]_[5] , _κd_ 12 gradients suffice). This prompts the question: can HMC attain complexity _independent_ of _d_ , assuming higher derivative bounds, from an explicit warm start? Theorem 4 answers this negatively (at least in terms of relaxation time) using an exponentially-sized bad set; moreover, our hard distribution is a Gaussian, with vanishing derivatives of order _≥_ 3.

**Lower bounds for sampling.** The bounds most closely relevant to those in this paper are given by [LST20a], who showed that the MALA step size must scale inversely in _κ_ for the chain to have a constant chance of moving, and [CLA[+] 20], who showed it must scale as _d[−]_ 2[1] . Theorem 2 matches or improves both bounds simultaneously while giving an explicit hard distribution and exp( _−d_ )-sized bad set. Moreover, both [LST20a, CLA[+] 20] gave strictly spectral lower bounds, complemented by our Theorem 3, a mixing time lower bound. We briefly mention several additional lower bound results in sampling-adjacent literature, related to this work. Recently, [CLW20] exhibited an information-theoretic lower bound on unadjusted discretizations simulating the _underdamped Langevin dynamics_ , whose dimension dependence matches the upper bound of [SL19] (leaving the precise _κ_ dependence open). Finally, [GLL20] and [CBL20] give information-theoretic lower bounds for estimating normalizing constants of well-conditioned distributions and the number of stochastic gradient queries required by first-order sampling under noisy gradient access respectively.

> 5There has been considerably more exploration of the unadjusted variant [MV18, MS19, BE21], which typically obtain mixing guarantees scaling polynomially in the inverse accuracy (as opposed to polylogarithmic).

## **3 Lower bounds for MALA**

In this section, we give the main constructions and technical tools we develop in proving Theorem 3. En route, we also summarize our proofs of Theorems 1 and 2. Our starting point is the observation (also made in [CLA[+] 20]) that for a MALA step size _h_ , the spectral gap of the MALA Markov chain scales no better than _O_ ( _h_ + _h_[2] ), witnessed by a simple one-dimensional Gaussian.

## **5 Conclusion**

We presented relaxation time lower bounds for the MALA and HMC Markov chains at every step size and scale, as well as a mixing time bound for MALA from an exponentially warm start. We highlight in this section a number of unexplored directions left open by our work, beyond direct strengthenings of our results, which we find interesting and defer to a future exploration.

**Variable or random step sizes.** The lower bounds of this paper were for MALA and HMC Markov chains with a _fixed step size_ . For variable step sizes which take e.g. values in a bounded multiplicative range, we believe our arguments can be modified to also give relaxation time lower bounds for the resulting Markov chains. However, the arguments of Section 4 (our HMC lower bound) are particularly brittle to large multiplicative ranges of candidate step sizes, because they rely on the locations of Chebyshev polynomial zeroes, which only occur in a bounded range. From an algorithm design perspective, this suggests that adaptively or randomly choosing step size ranges may be effective in improving the performance of HMC. Such a result would also give theoretical justification to the No-U-Turn sampler of [HG14], a common HMC alternative in practice. We state as an

9

explicit open problem: can one obtain improved upper bounds, such as a _[√] κ_ dependence or a dimension-independent rate, for example by using variations of these strategies (variable step sizes)?

**Necessity of** _κ_ **lower bound.** All of our witness sets throughout the paper are exp( _−d_ ) sized. It was observed in [DCWY18] that it is possible to construct a starting distribution with warmness arbitrarily close to _[√] κ[d]_ ; the marginal restriction of our witness set obtains this bound for all _κ ≥ e_[2] . However, recently [LST20b] proposed a _proximal point reduction_ for sampling, showing (for mixing bounds scaling at least linearly in _κ_ ) it suffices to sample a small number of regularized distributions, with conditioning arbitrarily close to 1. Adjusting constants, we can modify our Gaussian lower bounds (Theorems 1 and 4) to have witness sets with measure _c[d]_ for _c_ arbitrarily close to 1. However, our witness set for the family of hard non-Gaussian distributions encounters a natural barrier at measure 2 _[d]_ , as it is sign-restricted by the cosine function. We find it interesting to see if a stronger construction rules out existing warm starts for all _κ ≥_ 1, or if an upper bound can take advantage of the [LST20b] reduction to obtain improved dependences on dimension.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper addresses an important and understudied problem: establishing lower bounds for two widely-used sampling algorithms (MALA and HMC) in the well-conditioned regime, complementing an extensive line of upper-bound results. The contributions appear significant—a nearly-tight Ω(√κd/log d) relaxation-time lower bound for MALA on Gaussians that matches known upper bounds up to logarithmic factors, an extension to general well-conditioned densities, and a polynomial-in-dimension lower bound for HMC via a novel and intriguing connection between leapfrog integration and Chebyshev polynomials. The motivation is well-articulated, situating the work nicely within the broader analogy to optimization theory where matching upper/lower bounds exist, and the authors are appropriately candid about the scope limitation (these are algorithm-specific rather than unconditional oracle lower bounds). However, several clarity issues arise even in the introduction: the distinction between relaxation time and mixing time is flagged but its resolution is deferred, the role and necessity of the scale restriction [1,κ] is only gestured at via an appendix reference, and the assumption κ = O(d⁴) is introduced without adequate justification for readers unfamiliar with the comparison samplers cited. The abstract promises results "from an exponentially warm start" while Theorems 1–2 are stated in terms of relaxation time, and the relationship between these framings could be made more transparent upfront. Additionally, the practical implications—whether these lower bounds suggest MALA/HMC are fundamentally suboptimal or essentially optimal in this regime—could be foregrounded more clearly to help readers calibrate the significance of the contribution.

The summary states that the generic warm start N(x*, L^{-1}I) gives warmness κ^d, and §1.1 says 'It is unknown how to obtain a starting distribution ... with warmness better than κ^d'. Given the OCR-induced rendering, this may be a display issue, but the directionality of the claim is non-trivially confusing.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper appears to make a strong and technically significant contribution by addressing an important gap in the theory of sampling: lower bounds for widely used Metropolized methods in well-conditioned settings, with results that seem well positioned relative to known upper bounds and prior open questions. The motivation is clear and compelling, especially the contrast with optimization lower-bound theory and the focus on algorithm-specific barriers for MALA and HMC. The introduction does a good job contextualizing the relevance of well-conditioned distributions and explaining why MALA/HMC are natural targets. That said, the presentation is quite dense and assumes substantial familiarity with sampling theory, spectral gaps, warm starts, and Langevin discretizations; a broader NeurIPS audience may struggle to parse the precise implications of the theorems without more intuition. The theorem statements in the excerpt also appear visually corrupted or ambiguous in notation—e.g., the stated lower bounds contain malformed square roots/logarithms—so clarity of the quantitative claims should be checked carefully. The main high-level issue is that the introduction could more explicitly explain the hard instances and the qualitative meaning of the bounds: what feature makes MALA slow, how the Gaussian and non-Gaussian lower bounds differ, and why the HMC/Chebyshev connection matters beyond the technical proof. Overall, the paper seems high quality and potentially important, but its accessibility would benefit from cleaner notation, more interpretive discussion of the lower bounds, and a sharper roadmap distinguishing relaxation-time, mixing-time, and warm-start claims.

comments.title: HMC is described as discretizing Langevin dynamics rather than Hamiltonian dynamics
comments.quote: since seminal work of [JKO98], demonstrating that the Langevin dynamics which MALA and HMC discretize has an interpretation as gradient descent on density space
comments.explanation: The intended high-level point is understandable: MALA is obtained from an Euler discretization of overdamped Langevin diffusion, whose Wasserstein gradient-flow interpretation traces back to JKO, and HMC is another Metropolized gradient-based sampler. However, read literally, this sentence says that HMC discretizes the Langevin dynamics discussed by JKO. Standard multi-step HMC with leapfrog discretizes Hamiltonian dynamics on position-momentum space, with momentum refreshment, not overdamped Langevin diffusion. The special case K=1 can be identified with MALA after a parameter change, but for K>1 the dynamics are different. This is a localized introductory imprecision, but it could mislead readers about which continuous-time process the HMC analysis concerns.

comments.title: Abstract may overstate the HMC lower bound as a dimension-dependent relaxation-time bound for any step count
comments.quote: We also show that a polynomial dependence on dimension is necessary for the relaxation time of HMC under any number of leapfrog steps, and bound the gains achievable by changing the step count.
comments.explanation: The later theorem appears to lower bound the relaxation time roughly by Ω~(κ√d/K). Since each HMC iteration uses K gradients, this implies a polynomial-in-d lower bound in gradient-query complexity, but the relaxation time in iterations can lose its polynomial d-dependence if K is allowed to scale sufficiently with d. Thus the abstract's phrase that polynomial dependence on dimension is necessary for the relaxation time under any number of leapfrog steps is stronger than what the formal statement supports unless K is held fixed or the intended cost measure is gradient queries. This could be fixed by saying polynomial dependence is necessary in query complexity, or by explicitly qualifying the relaxation-time statement with its K-dependence.

comments.title: Warm-start lower bounds are sometimes phrased as stronger explicit-start conclusions
comments.quote:
Our main result is a nearly-tight lower bound of Ω([] _κad_ ) on the mixing time of MALA from an exponentially warm start, matching a line of algorithmic results [DCWY18, CDWY19, LST20a] up to logarithmic factors and answering an open question of [CLA[+] 20].

We address this question via Theorem 3, showing the _O_[] ( _κad_ ) rate of [LST20a] for MALA applied to a _κ_ -conditioned density is nearly tight. ... It also implies going beyond _O_[] ( _κad_ ) mixing for general well-conditioned densities requires subexponential warmness.
comments.explanation: The paper later explains that the lower bound is warmness-sensitive and that both the hard density and the starting distribution may be chosen adversarially based on the algorithm parameters. That establishes that no guarantee depending only on exp(d)-warmness can uniformly beat the stated rate over all such starts. It does not by itself rule out the possibility that a particular explicit exp(d)-warm initialization, with additional structure beyond its warmness parameter, could avoid the bad set and mix faster on all targets in the class. Therefore abstract-level claims about answering an explicit-start open question, or statements that improving beyond the stated rate requires subexponential warmness, should be qualified as applying to worst-case warm starts or warmness-only guarantees.

comments.title: Lower-bound statement is phrased using upper-bound notation
comments.quote: We demonstrate that HMC cannot obtain a better relaxation time than _O_[] ( _κ√dK[−]_[1] ) for any _K_ , even when the target is a Gaussian.
comments.explanation: The next theorem clarifies the intended mathematical statement: the relaxation time is lower bounded, up to logarithmic factors, by roughly κ√d/K. But the quoted sentence uses O~(·), which conventionally denotes an upper bound, in a sentence describing an impossibility or lower bound. A careful reader can infer the intended meaning as “cannot be better than order κ√d/K,” but the notation is technically imprecise and could be misread as claiming an upper bound on HMC's relaxation time. Replacing it with Ω~(κ√d/K), or saying “better scaling than κ√d/K up to logarithmic factors,” would avoid the ambiguity.

comments.title: “Step size” appears to refer to the HMC step count K
comments.quote: In particular, we demonstrate that if _K ≤ d[c]_ for some constant _c_ , then _K_ -step HMC can only improve the relaxation time of Theorem 4 by roughly _K_[2] , showing that ... to truly go beyond a _κad_ relaxation time by a _d_[Ω(1)] factor, the step size must scale polynomially with the dimension.
comments.explanation: The discussion is about the number of leapfrog steps K: the preceding sentence conditions on K ≤ d^c and describes the gains from increasing K. In that context, the phrase “the step size must scale polynomially with the dimension” is confusing, because “step size” elsewhere denotes the leapfrog discretization parameter η, not the step count K. The intended statement seems to be that the step count must be polynomially large, or at least grow as d^{Ω(1)}, to obtain a polynomial improvement. If the authors really mean the numerical step size η, that would require additional explanation.

comments.title: Increasing the leapfrog count does not by itself make the discretization more faithful
comments.quote: For larger _K_ , the algorithm simulates the (continuous-time) Hamiltonian dynamics with respect to the potential _f_ ( _x_ ) +[1] 2 _[∥][v][∥]_ 2[2][where] _[ f]_[is] the target’s negative log-density and _v_ is an auxiliary “velocity;” intuitively, larger _K_ leads to more faithful discretizations.
comments.explanation: Multi-step HMC uses more leapfrog steps and can produce longer Hamiltonian-like proposals than one-step MALA. However, for fixed step size η, increasing K increases the integration time T=Kη; it does not refine the discretization of a fixed-time Hamiltonian trajectory. Leapfrog accuracy over a fixed interval is controlled by decreasing η, while for fixed η the accumulated global error typically grows with trajectory length, before stability considerations. Thus “larger K leads to more faithful discretizations” is potentially misleading unless the comparison holds total integration time fixed or also adjusts η. The formal results later depend on both η and K, so this is a framing issue rather than a proof error.

comments.title: Relaxation-time lower bound does not fully answer the explicit-warm-start mixing question
comments.quote: This prompts the question: can HMC attain complexity _independent_ of _d_ , assuming higher derivative bounds, from an explicit warm start? Theorem 4 answers this negatively (at least in terms of relaxation time) using an exponentially-sized bad set; moreover, our hard distribution is a Gaussian, with vanishing derivatives of order _≥_ 3.
comments.explanation: The qualifier “at least in terms of relaxation time” is helpful, and Theorem 4 is relevant evidence against dimension-independent HMC performance. But the motivating question is phrased as a warm-start mixing question, whereas Theorem 4 is a spectral/relaxation-time lower bound and does not specify an explicit starting distribution or prove a total-variation mixing lower bound from such a start. Earlier in the paper the authors emphasize that, in continuous spaces, relaxation time and mixing time from a specified start are not automatically interchangeable. Therefore the theorem does not literally answer the stated explicit-warm-start question; it answers a related spectral version of it.

comments.quote:
These calculations imply that from this warm start, the rejection probability is exp( _−_ Ω( _h_[2] _κ_[2] _d_ )), which implies any _h_ = _ω_ ( _[√]_ log _d· κ_ ~~_√_~~ 1 _d_[)] cannot leave the set with high probability; combined with Lemma 1, this proves Theorem 1.

where the contents of the expectation in (6) are the log-rejection probability along one coordinate by a straightforward calculation.
comments.explanation: The surrounding argument is clear in intent: a negative upper bound on the Metropolis log-acceptance ratio should imply that proposed moves are almost always rejected, so the chain remains trapped in the witness set. But the probability of accepting, not rejecting, is the quantity of order exp(-Ω(h²κ²d)) or exp(-Ω(hκd)) in the analogous later discussion. The rejection probability should be close to 1 minus that small acceptance probability. The phrase “log-rejection probability” is also nonstandard and appears to mean the log acceptance ratio. Read literally, the current wording reverses the sign logic: an exponentially small rejection probability would mean the chain almost always moves, contradicting the conclusion that it cannot leave the set with high probability.

comments.title: Conductance direction is described backwards
comments.quote: this implies a _conductance_ lower bound with the given witness set, which yields a spectral gap bound by the well-known Cheeger’s inequality [Che69].
comments.explanation: The intended bottleneck argument is to exhibit a set S of stationary mass at least exp(-d) such that, from points in S, the chain leaves S with very small probability. This gives a small-conductance witness, i.e. an upper bound on the conductance Φ, and then a Cheeger-type inequality gives an upper bound on the spectral gap and hence a lower bound on relaxation time. Calling this a “conductance lower bound” reverses the usual direction, since lower-bounding conductance would normally support fast mixing rather than a lower-bound construction.

comments.title: Conditioning on the witness set is described with ambiguous “marginal” terminology and warmness comparison
comments.quote:

comments.title: Chebyshev extrema are not exactly the zeroes of U_{k-1} unless endpoints are excluded
comments.quote: Magically, the extremal points in [ _−_ 1 _,_ 1] where _Tk_ is _±_ 1 are _exactly the same_ as the zeroes of _Uk−_ 1.
comments.explanation: The underlying connection is standard and essentially correct: T_k'(x)=kU_{k-1}(x), so the interior extrema of T_k occur at the zeroes of U_{k-1}. But the statement as written says the extremal points in [-1,1] exactly coincide with those zeroes, which is false if endpoints are included: T_k also attains extrema at x=±1, while U_{k-1}(±1) is nonzero. Later indexing suggests the authors mean only the interior extrema, so the fix is simply to narrow the wording to “interior extremal points.”

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper appears to make a strong and technically significant contribution by addressing an important gap in the theory of sampling: lower bounds for widely used Metropolized methods in well-conditioned settings, with results that seem well positioned relative to known upper bounds and prior open questions. The motivation is clear and compelling, especially the contrast with optimization lower-bound theory and the focus on algorithm-specific barriers for MALA and HMC. The introduction does a good job contextualizing the relevance of well-conditioned distributions and explaining why MALA/HMC are natural targets. That said, the presentation is quite dense and assumes substantial familiarity with sampling theory, spectral gaps, warm starts, and Langevin discretizations; a broader NeurIPS audience may struggle to parse the precise implications of the theorems without more intuition. The theorem statements in the excerpt also appear visually corrupted or ambiguous in notation—e.g., the stated lower bounds contain malformed square roots/logarithms—so clarity of the quantitative claims should be checked carefully. The main high-level issue is that the introduction could more explicitly explain the hard instances and the qualitative meaning of the bounds: what feature makes MALA slow, how the Gaussian and non-Gaussian lower bounds differ, and why the HMC/Chebyshev connection matters beyond the technical proof. Overall, the paper seems high quality and potentially important, but its accessibility would benefit from cleaner notation, more interpretive discussion of the lower bounds, and a sharper roadmap distinguishing relaxation-time, mixing-time, and warm-start claims.

comments.title: HMC is described as discretizing Langevin dynamics rather than Hamiltonian dynamics
comments.quote: since seminal work of [JKO98], demonstrating that the Langevin dynamics which MALA and HMC discretize has an interpretation as gradient descent on density space
comments.explanation: I understand the intended high-level point: both MALA and HMC are Metropolized gradient-based samplers, and MALA in particular is obtained from an Euler discretization of the overdamped Langevin diffusion, whose Wasserstein gradient-flow interpretation traces back to JKO. The later preliminaries also correctly define HMC through leapfrog steps for Hamilton's equations. However, read literally, this sentence says that HMC discretizes the Langevin dynamics discussed by JKO. Standard multi-step HMC with leapfrog discretizes Hamiltonian dynamics on position-momentum space, with momentum refreshment, not the overdamped Langevin diffusion. The special case K=1 can be identified with MALA after a parameter change, but for K>1 the formal dynamics are different. This is a localized introductory imprecision, but it could mislead readers about which continuous-time process the HMC analysis concerns.

comments.title: Abstract may overstate the HMC lower bound as a dimension-dependent relaxation-time bound for any step count
comments.quote: We also show that a polynomial dependence on dimension is necessary for the relaxation time of HMC under any number of leapfrog steps, and bound the gains achievable by changing the step count.
comments.explanation: The intended message is clear from the surrounding context: increasing the leapfrog count K cannot eliminate dimension dependence in the overall gradient-query complexity, and the paper studies how much improvement in iteration complexity is possible as K changes. But the more precise statement later is of the form that the relaxation time is lower bounded roughly by \(\widetilde{\Omega}(\kappa \sqrt d / K)\). Since each HMC iteration uses K gradients, this implies a polynomial-in-d lower bound in gradient queries, but the relaxation time in iterations itself can lose its polynomial d-dependence if K is allowed to scale sufficiently with d. Thus the abstract's phrase 'polynomial dependence on dimension is necessary for the relaxation time ... under any number of leapfrog steps' is stronger than what the later theorem appears to support unless K is being held fixed or the cost measure is gradient queries. This is fixable by saying polynomial dependence is necessary in query complexity, or by explicitly qualifying the relaxation-time statement with its K-dependence.

comments.title: The warm-start lower bound is stated more broadly than the formal worst-case warm-start result
comments.quote: Our main result is a nearly-tight lower bound of Ω([] _κd_ ) on the mixing time of MALA from an exponentially warm start, matching a line of algorithmic results [DCWY18, CDWY19, LST20a] up to logarithmic factors and answering an open question of [CLA[+] 20].
comments.explanation: The broad comparison to prior upper bounds is understandable: the paper later proves a mixing-time lower bound with an \(\exp(d)\)-warm initialization, and this is the right scale for many conductance-style warm-start analyses. The later text also candidly explains that the lower bound is warmness-sensitive and that both the hard density and the starting distribution may be chosen adversarially based on the algorithm parameters. What remains potentially misleading here is the phrase 'from an exponentially warm start' together with 'answering an open question' about explicit starts. An existential/adversarial exponentially warm start shows that warmness alone cannot guarantee faster mixing from all exponential-warm starts, but it does not by itself rule out the possibility that some other explicit initialization for the same target family could avoid the bad set and mix faster. The later discussion partially resolves this by specifying the non-standard lower-bound form, but the abstract-level formulation could be read as settling the stronger explicit-start question outright.

comments.title: Theorem 3 only rules out worst-case warm starts, not all explicit exponentially warm starts
comments.quote: We address this question via Theorem 3, showing the _O_[] ( _κd_ ) rate of [LST20a] for MALA applied to a _κ_ -conditioned density is nearly tight. ... It also implies going beyond _O_[] ( _κd_ ) mixing for general well-conditioned densities requires subexponential warmness.
comments.explanation: The surrounding paragraph correctly explains that Theorem 3 gives a mixing-time lower bound from an exp(d)-warm start, and the later paragraph helpfully acknowledges the non-standard, warmness-sensitive form: the hard density and starting distribution may both be chosen adaptively based on the algorithm parameters. That context resolves part of the concern. However, the quoted conclusion seems stronger than what this form of lower bound establishes. A lower bound for an adversarially chosen exp(d)-warm start shows that no guarantee depending only on exp(d)-warmness can beat the stated rate uniformly over all such starts. It does not by itself rule out a particular explicit exp(d)-warm start, with additional structure beyond its warmness parameter, from mixing faster on all well-conditioned targets. Thus the statement that going beyond \(\widetilde O(\kappa d)\) “requires subexponential warmness” should be qualified as applying to worst-case warm-start or warmness-only guarantees.

comments.title: Lower-bound statement is phrased using upper-bound notation
comments.quote: We demonstrate that HMC cannot obtain a better relaxation time than _O_[] ( _κ√dK[−]_[1] ) for any _K_ , even when the target is a Gaussian.
comments.explanation: The next theorem clarifies the intended mathematical statement: the relaxation time is lower bounded, up to logarithmic factors, by roughly \(\kappa\sqrt d/K\). So the surrounding context makes the authors’ intent clear. Still, the quoted sentence uses \(\widetilde O(\cdot)\), which conventionally denotes an upper bound, in a sentence describing an impossibility/lower bound. A careful reader can infer the intended meaning as “cannot be better than order \(\kappa\sqrt d/K\),” but the notation is technically imprecise and could be misread as claiming an upper bound on HMC’s relaxation time. Replacing it with \(\widetilde\Omega(\kappa\sqrt d/K)\), or saying “better scaling than \(\kappa\sqrt d/K\) up to logarithmic factors,” would avoid the ambiguity.

comments.title: “Step size” appears to refer to the HMC step count \(K\)
comments.quote:
In particular, we demonstrate that if _K ≤ d[c]_ for some constant _c_ , then _K_ -step HMC can only improve the relaxation time of Theorem 4 by roughly _K_[2] , showing that

> 3The warmness of a distribution is the worst-case ratio between measures it and the stationary assign to a set.

> 4As discussed, it is currently unknown how to obtain such a warm start generically.

3

to truly go beyond a _κd_ relaxation time by a _d_[Ω(1)] factor, the step size must scale polynomially with the dimension.
comments.explanation: The discussion is about the number of leapfrog steps \(K\), and the preceding sentence explicitly conditions on \(K \le d^c\) and describes the gains from increasing \(K\). In that context, the final phrase “the step size must scale polynomially with the dimension” is confusing, because “step size” elsewhere denotes the leapfrog discretization parameter \(\eta\), not the step count \(K\). The intended statement seems to be that the step count must be polynomially large, or at least grow as \(d^{\Omega(1)}\), to obtain a polynomial improvement. If the authors really mean the numerical step size \(\eta\), that would require additional explanation, since it does not follow directly from the stated comparison in terms of \(K\).

comments.title: One-step HMC equals MALA only after a step-size reparameterization
comments.quote: HMC can be viewed as a multi-step generalization of MALA, with two parameters (a step size _η_ and a step count _K_ ); when _K_ = 1, it matches MALA exactly.
comments.explanation:
The intended statement is essentially correct, but I had to check the parameterization to make it true. With one leapfrog step, HMC proposes \(y=x+\eta v-(\eta^2/2)
abla f(x)\), so marginally \(y\sim N(x-(\eta^2/2)
abla f(x),\eta^2 I)\). This matches the MALA proposal \(N(x-h
abla f(x),2hI)\) only after setting \(h=\eta^2/2\). The Metropolis acceptance rule also agrees under this identification, using the final momentum expression. Thus the authors are right about the specialization, but the sentence as written can be read as equality at the same numerical step-size parameter, which is not correct. A parenthetical such as “with \(h=\eta^2/2\)” would remove the ambiguity.

comments.title: Increasing the leapfrog count does not by itself make the discretization more faithful
comments.quote: For larger _K_ , the algorithm simulates the (continuous-time) Hamiltonian dynamics with respect to the potential _f_ ( _x_ ) +[1] 2 _[∥][v][∥]_ 2[2][where] _[ f]_[is] the target’s negative log-density and _v_ is an auxiliary “velocity;” intuitively, larger _K_ leads to more faithful discretizations.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended intuition: multi-step HMC uses more leapfrog steps and can produce longer, more Hamiltonian-like proposals than one-step MALA. However, for fixed step size \(\eta\), increasing \(K\) increases the integration time \(T=K\eta\); it does not refine the discretization of a fixed-time Hamiltonian trajectory. Leapfrog accuracy over a fixed interval is controlled by decreasing \(\eta\), while for fixed \(\eta\) the accumulated global error typically grows with trajectory length, e.g. on the order of \(K\eta^3\) in standard smooth settings before stability considerations. Thus “larger \(K\) leads to more faithful discretizations” is potentially misleading unless the comparison holds total integration time fixed or also adjusts \(\eta\). The formal results later depend on both \(\eta\) and \(K\), so this is a framing issue rather than a proof error.

comments.title: Relaxation-time lower bound does not fully answer the explicit-warm-start mixing question
comments.quote: This prompts the question: can HMC attain complexity _independent_ of _d_ , assuming higher derivative bounds, from an explicit warm start? Theorem 4 answers this negatively (at least in terms of relaxation time) using an exponentially-sized bad set; moreover, our hard distribution is a Gaussian, with vanishing derivatives of order _≥_ 3.
comments.explanation: The qualifier “at least in terms of relaxation time” is helpful, and Theorem 4 is indeed relevant evidence against dimension-independent HMC performance. But the motivating question is phrased as a warm-start mixing question, whereas Theorem 4 is a spectral/relaxation-time lower bound and does not specify an explicit starting distribution or prove a total-variation mixing lower bound from such a start. Earlier in the paper the authors themselves emphasize that, in continuous spaces, relaxation time and mixing time from a specified start are not automatically interchangeable. Therefore the theorem does not literally answer the stated explicit-warm-start question; it answers a related spectral version of it. This could be fixed by slightly weakening the prose, e.g. saying that Theorem 4 “rules out the corresponding dimension-independent relaxation-time guarantee.”

comments.title: Acceptance and rejection probabilities appear to be swapped
comments.quote: These calculations imply that from this warm start, the rejection probability is exp( _−_ Ω( _h_[2] _κ_[2] _d_ )), which implies any _h_ = _ω_ ( _[√]_ log _d· κ_ ~~_√_~~ 1 _d_[)] cannot leave the set with high probability; combined with Lemma 1, this proves Theorem 1.
comments.explanation: The surrounding argument is clear in intent: Lemma 2 gives a negative upper bound on the log Metropolis acceptance ratio of order \(-h^2\kappa^2 d\), so for large enough \(h\) proposed moves should almost always be rejected and the chain should remain trapped in the witness set. But the probability of accepting, not rejecting, is the quantity of order \(\exp(-\Omega(h^2\kappa^2 d))\). The rejection probability should be close to \(1-\exp(-\Omega(h^2\kappa^2 d))\), or at least \(1-\mathrm{poly}(d^{-1})\) in the regime being ruled out. This is not just a wording preference: as written, an exponentially small rejection probability would mean the chain almost always moves, contradicting the conclusion that it cannot leave the set with high probability. The same apparent swap recurs later in the Theorem 2 discussion, where a log-acceptance bound of order \(-\Omega(h\kappa d)\) is described as making the “rejection probability” \(\exp(-\Omega(h\kappa d))\).

comments.title: Conductance direction is described backwards
comments.quote: this implies a _conductance_ lower bound with the given witness set, which yields a spectral gap bound by the well-known Cheeger’s inequality [Che69].
comments.explanation: I understand the intended bottleneck argument: exhibit a set \(S\) of stationary mass at least \(\exp(-d)\) such that, from points in \(S\), the chain leaves \(S\) with very small probability. This gives a small-conductance witness, i.e. an upper bound on the conductance \(\Phi\), and then the standard Cheeger-type inequality for reversible Markov chains gives an upper bound on the spectral gap, hence a lower bound on relaxation time. Calling this a “conductance lower bound” reverses the usual direction. The intended conclusion is recoverable from context, but the phrasing could mislead readers because lower-bounding conductance would normally support fast mixing rather than a spectral-gap lower-bound construction.

comments.title: The norm condition in the Gaussian discussion seems directionally inconsistent
comments.quote: From this point, we observe that as long as _∥x∥_[2] 2[is at least a constant factor away from its expectation] _d_ ( _≈ κ_[),][the][dominant][behavior][of][(5)][scales][as] _[−][h]_[2] _[κ]_[2] _[d]_[.]
comments.explanation: Some of this line is clearly affected by OCR: for the quadratic hard instance \(f_{hq}(x)=\frac12 x_1^2+\frac\kappa2\|x_{-1}\|^2\), the typical squared norm of the high-curvature coordinates is of order \(d/\kappa\), not “\(d\) (\(\approx\kappa\))”. Interpreting the corrupted text as \(d/\kappa\) resolves the scale issue. However, even under that charitable reading, the phrase “at least a constant factor away from its expectation” appears reversed relative to the next sentence, where the chosen small-ball set is intended to contain typical points, roughly \(\|x\|^2\lesssim d/\kappa\), not points bounded away from typicality. The argument seems to require \(\|x\|^2\) to be within a constant-factor upper bound of its expectation, so that the norm-dependent terms do not dominate the \(-h^2\kappa^2 d\) behavior. This is likely a localized wording/OCR issue, but as written it points in the wrong direction.

comments.title: “Log-rejection probability” is nonstandard and appears to mean log-acceptance ratio
comments.quote: where the contents of the expectation in (6) are the log-rejection probability along one coordinate by a straightforward calculation.
comments.explanation: The preceding and following discussion concerns showing that the Metropolis log-acceptance ratio is negative with magnitude \(\Omega(h\kappa d)\), so that acceptance is rare. In Metropolis-Hastings terminology, the quantity usually computed is the log acceptance ratio, \(\log(\pi(y)q_y(x)/(\pi(x)q_x(y)))\), sometimes clipped at zero for the actual log acceptance probability. A “log-rejection probability” is not a standard object, and if read literally it would have the opposite monotonic interpretation: a large negative log-rejection probability would mean rejection is unlikely. The intended calculation is still understandable from context, especially because the authors repeatedly discuss log-acceptance elsewhere, but this terminology reinforces the acceptance/rejection swap noted above and could confuse the sign logic of the lower bound.

comments.title: Lemma 5 as stated only gives a little-o time barrier, not an \(\Omega\) mixing lower bound
comments.quote:
By initializing at the exp( _d_ )-warm start _∥x∥_[2] 2 _[≤]_[1] 2 _[d]_[ (the “small ball” from Theorem 1’s proof), and combining with our] lower bounds on the other ranges of _h_ , we conclude our proof of Theorem 3.

**Lemma 5** (Lemma 11, supplement) **.** ... _With]_ 99 _κd probability at least_ 100 _[, both of the following events occur for][ T]_[=] _[ o]_  log[2] _d_  _:
comments.explanation: The intended argument is clear: start from a small-ball warm start, show that for many steps the chain remains in a set of exponentially small stationary measure, and hence total variation remains large. That is the right mechanism for Theorem 3. However, as stated here, Lemma 5 appears to hold for \(T=o(\kappa d/\log^2 d)\) rather than for all \(T\le c\kappa d/\log^2 d\) with a fixed positive constant \(c\). A little-o statement rules out mixing in times asymptotically smaller than \(\kappa d/\log^2 d\), but by itself it does not imply an \(\Omega(\kappa d/\log^2 d)\) lower bound in the standard sense; the true mixing time could still be, for example, \(\kappa d/\log^3 d\). This may be strengthened in the supplement, and the OCR has clearly garbled the displayed fraction, but the logical gap remains in the passage as written because it explicitly says this lemma concludes Theorem 3.

comments.title: The “marginal on” the small ball is not a well-defined start distribution
comments.quote: _Consider iterating the MALA Markov chain with h_ = _O_ (log _d ·_ 1 1 _κd_[)] _[on][N]_[(0] _[,]_ **[ I]**[)] _[for][T][iterations,][with][starting][distribution][the][marginal][on][∥][x][∥]_[2] 2 _[≤]_ 2 _[d][.]
comments.explanation: The intended starting distribution is almost certainly the target Gaussian conditioned on the event \(\|x\|_2^2\le d/2\), which is indeed exponentially warm because the conditioning event has probability \(\exp(-O(d))\). The surrounding prose calling it the “small ball” supports this reading. Still, “the marginal on \(\|x\|_2^2\le d/2\)” is not standard terminology: a marginal refers to projecting onto coordinates, whereas here the needed object is a conditional/restricted distribution. This matters because warmness and the total variation argument depend on the exact initial law. A careful reader can infer the authors’ intent, but the formal lemma statement should say “the distribution \(N(0,I)\) conditioned on \(\|x\|_2^2\le d/2\)” or equivalent.

comments.title: Chebyshev extrema are not exactly the zeroes of \(U_{k-1}\) unless endpoints are excluded
comments.quote: Magically, the extremal points in [ _−_ 1 _,_ 1] where _Tk_ is _±_ 1 are _exactly the same_ as the zeroes of _Uk−_ 1.
comments.explanation: The connection being used is standard and basically correct: \(T_k'(x)=kU_{k-1}(x)\), so the interior extrema of \(T_k\) occur at the zeroes of \(U_{k-1}\). This is precisely what is useful for the HMC resonance construction. But the statement as written says “the extremal points in \([-1,1]\)” exactly coincide with the zeroes, which is false if endpoints are included: \(T_k\) also attains extrema at \(x=\pm1\), while \(U_{k-1}(\pm1)\neq0\). Later the authors use indices \(j\in[K-1]\), suggesting they do mean only the interior extrema, so the mathematical idea is sound. The wording should be narrowed to “interior extremal points” to avoid a misleading exact claim.

comments.title: Ambiguous comparison between the conditioned witness-set start and the \(\kappa^{d/2}\)-warm start
comments.quote: It was observed in [DCWY18] that it is possible to construct a starting distribution with warmness arbitrarily close to _[√] κ[d]_ ; the marginal restriction of our witness set obtains this bound for all _κ ≥ e_[2] .
comments.explanation: I read the intended bound as the standard \(\kappa^{d/2}\) warmness of the Gaussian start from the mode. Given the preceding sentence that the witness sets have stationary measure about \(\exp(-d)\), conditioning \(\pi\) on such a set gives warmness about \(\exp(d)\), so for \(\kappa \ge e^2\) this is indeed at most \(\kappa^{d/2}\). Thus the underlying comparison seems correct. What remains slightly problematic is the wording “obtains this bound”: the conditioned witness-set distribution does not generally have warmness arbitrarily close to \(\kappa^{d/2}\); it is only bounded by that quantity, with equality only at the threshold scale. Also, “marginal restriction” is not a standard phrase here and could be read as something other than conditioning the stationary distribution on the witness set. A more precise phrasing would avoid possible confusion, e.g. that conditioning on the witness set is \(\le \kappa^{d/2}\)-warm for \(\kappa\ge e^2\).
